title: Psalms 36
subtitle: Your Love Reaches to the Heavens

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “There Is No Fear of God Before Their Eyes”

heb.paragraph: "An oracle is within my heart concerning the sinfulness of the wicked: there is no fear of God before his eyes" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Romans 3:18. The wicked person’s fundamental problem: no reverence for God. Without fear, sin has no brake.

notes.note: MacArthur: Paul quotes this verse in Romans 3:18 as the summary indictment of human sinfulness. The absence of the fear of God is the root cause of all the sins listed in Romans 3:10-17.
MacArthur: the deliberate planning of evil shows that human depravity is not merely occasional weakness but a settled disposition. Sin is not an accident but a lifestyle choice when God is excluded.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the opening is unusual — an oracle (ne'um) about wickedness, a genre typically reserved for divine speech. The psalmist receives a prophetic insight into the inner life of the wicked: no fear of God before his eyes.

Alter: 'they flatter themselves too much to detect or hate their sin' — self-deception is the wicked person's defining trait. They have lost the capacity for honest self-evaluation. Sin has become invisible to the sinner.

Calvin: 'no fear of God before their eyes' — Calvin identifies this as the root sin, the fountainhead from which all other wickedness flows. Without the fear of God, moral restraint dissolves completely.

Calvin: planning evil on one's bed — Calvin notes that wickedness is not impulsive but deliberate. The wicked premeditate their cruelty in private before executing it in public. Evil requires intention.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: ne'um ('oracle') is usually followed by 'of the Lord' (as in prophetic speech). Here it is 'oracle of transgression' — sin itself speaks to the wicked. Personified rebellion functions as a false prophet whispering counsel.

NET: 'no fear of God before his eyes' — paḥad is not mere reverence but dread, the awareness that one stands accountable before a holy God. Its absence removes the last moral check on human behaviour.

Kidner: the psalm opens not with God but with the godless. The portrait of wickedness (vv.1-4) serves as a dark backdrop against which God's love (vv.5-9) shines all the brighter. Structure serves theology.
Kidner: self-flattery prevents self-knowledge. The wicked person is not struggling against sin but blind to it. Kidner: this is worse than defiance — it is the inability to see what one has become.

VanGemeren: the 'oracle of transgression' inverts the prophetic oracle. Where God's word brings truth, sin's word brings delusion. The wicked are not merely disobedient but deceived — listening to the wrong voice.

VanGemeren: 'commits to a sinful course' — the Hebrew suggests deliberate resolve, not weakness. The wicked have made a decision: they will not reject evil. This is chosen darkness, not accidental stumbling.

Goldingay: the Hebrew is notoriously difficult. 'Oracle of transgression to the wicked in the midst of his heart' — sin speaks to the wicked person internally, as God speaks to the prophet. Transgression has its own prophetic voice.

Goldingay: the progression is chilling: deceptive speech (v.3a), ceasing to be wise or do good (v.3b), plotting evil in bed (v.4a), committing to wrong (v.4b). The descent from words to plans to commitment traces sin's escalation.

sections.header: Verses 5–12 — “Your Faithfulness Reaches to the Skies”

heb.paragraph: "Your love (ḥesed), LORD, reaches to the heavens, your faithfulness to the skies" (v.5). The psalm’s theology: God’s love is cosmic in scale. It reaches the heavens; his faithfulness touches the clouds; his righteousness is like great mountains; his justice like the great deep (vv.5–6). "How priceless is your unfailing love, God! People take refuge in the shadow of your wings" (v.7).

notes.note: MacArthur: the vastness of God's attributes — heavens, skies, mountains, deep — reminds believers that God's love and justice operate on a scale beyond human comprehension. His mercy is as reliable as creation itself.

MacArthur: 'in your light we see light' — apart from God's self-revelation, human beings cannot perceive spiritual truth. This verse undergirds the doctrine of illumination: the Spirit enables understanding of Scripture.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the cosmic scale of God's attributes — love reaching to heavens, faithfulness to the skies, righteousness like mountains, justice like the deep — contrasts starkly with the cramped inner world of the wicked in vv.1-4.

Alter: 'in your light we see light' — one of the most profound theological statements in the Psalter. Light is not self-generated; it is received. We see reality only by the illumination God provides.

Calvin: four divine attributes (love, faithfulness, righteousness, justice) are measured against four cosmic realities (heavens, skies, mountains, deep). Calvin: God's character is as vast and permanent as creation itself.

Calvin: 'the fountain of life' — Calvin reads this as the definitive statement on human dependence. We do not generate life; we receive it. We do not create truth; we see it by God's light. All is gift.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'your justice like the great deep' (tehôm rabbāh) — tehôm is the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2. God's justice is as vast and unfathomable as the ocean abyss. It cannot be exhausted or fully comprehended.
NET: 'river of your delights' (naḥal 'adāneyḵā) — 'adānîm is related to 'Eden.' The river of God's delights echoes the rivers of Eden (Gen 2:10). The temple experience recaptures paradise.

Kidner: five verses celebrate God's love, and each image outdoes the last: heavens, skies, mountains, deep, then the intimate images of refuge, feasting, drinking, and light. From cosmic to personal in five steps.

Kidner: 'fountain of life…in your light we see light' — the twin metaphors of water and light capture everything: water sustains biological life, light enables intellectual and spiritual perception. God is the source of both.

VanGemeren: the temple imagery — refuge, feasting, the river of delights — anticipates the eschatological vision of Revelation 22. The river of life flowing from God's throne fulfils what the psalmist experiences in shadow.
VanGemeren: 'in your light we see light' — John 1:4-5 and 1 John 1:5 develop this theology. God is light; in him there is no darkness. The psalmist's metaphor becomes Johannine christology.

Goldingay: the four cosmic metaphors are not mere hyperbole. They make a theological claim: God's character is woven into the structure of reality. Love, faithfulness, righteousness, justice — these are the universe's foundations.

Goldingay: 'fountain of life' — the image works on multiple levels: physical survival (water), spiritual vitality (life), and epistemological clarity (light). The psalm's deepest verse is also its most economical.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Your Love Reaches to the Heavens.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in Your Love Reaches to the Heavens.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: Solomon is a key figure in Your Love Reaches to the Heavens.

ppl.text: Abraham is a key figure in Your Love Reaches to the Heavens.

ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in Your Love Reaches to the Heavens.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI have a message from God in my heart concerning the sinfulness of the wicked: There is no fear of God before their eyes.ESVTransgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes.NIVHow priceless is your unfailing love, O God! People take refuge in the shadow of your wings.ESVHow precious is your steadfast love, O God! The children of mankind take refuge in the shadow of your wings.

rows.label: No Fear — 36:1–4
rows.text: "No fear of God before their eyes." The root of wickedness: absent reverence.

hebtext.note: "An oracle is within my heart concerning the sinfulness of the wicked: there is no fear of God before his eyes" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Romans 3:18. The wicked person’s fundamental problem: no reverence for God. Without fear, sin has no brake.

"Your love (ḥesed), LORD, reaches to the heavens, your faithfulness to the skies" (v.5). The psalm’s theology: God’s love is cosmic in scale. It reaches the heavens; his faithfulness touches the clouds; his righteousness is like great mountains; his justice like the great deep (vv.5–6). "How priceless is your unfailing love, God! People take refuge in the shadow of your wings" (v.7).

themes.note: Mercy peaks: cosmic-scale ḥesed. Holiness: the root of wickedness is absent reverence. Sovereignty: mountains, ocean, heavens as measures of God’s character.
